%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand what element-by-element operations are in MATLAB, why they are necessary for vector computations, and how to apply them correctly.

In MATLAB, signals like \(f(t)\) and \(g(t)\) are stored as row vectors of sampled values. When you want to multiply two signals point-by-point — for example, to apply an exponential envelope \(g(t) = e^{-10t}\) to a sinusoid \(f(t)\) — you cannot use standard matrix multiplication. Instead, MATLAB requires the **dot operator** (e.g., `.*`) to perform element-by-element multiplication. This section explains why that distinction matters, how to write it correctly, and what the result looks like graphically. After this section, you will be able to write MATLAB code that multiplies two same-length vectors sample-by-sample and interpret the resulting waveform.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.14](/figures/page-049-fig__b_14-1.png)
*Fig. B.14 shows \(f(t)\) (a pure sinusoid) and \(h(t) = f(t) \cdot g(t)\) (the exponentially enveloped result) on the same axes, making it immediately visible that element-by-element multiplication shapes the amplitude of one signal using another.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Element-by-element multiplication (.*) pairs each sample of \(f\) with the corresponding sample of \(g\) to produce \(h\); using * instead triggers a dimension error or incorrect matrix result.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777217329438-9022.png)

## Core Idea

When MATLAB stores a signal, it stores it as a **vector of sampled values**. For example, if \(t\) is a row vector of 1000 time points, then \(f(t)\) and \(g(t)\) are also row vectors of 1000 numbers.

To compute \(h(t) = f(t) \cdot g(t)\) — multiplying the two signals sample-by-sample — you must write:

```matlab
h = f .* g;
```

The dot before `*` tells MATLAB: **do not treat these as matrices; instead multiply position 1 with position 1, position 2 with position 2, and so on.**

### WHY THE DOT MATTERS

Without the dot, `f * g` attempts **matrix multiplication**, which either throws a dimension error (both are row vectors) or produces a scalar inner product — neither of which gives you the signal \(h(t)\).

### EXAM TRAP

The most common mistake is writing `h = f * g` instead of `h = f .* g`. The same rule applies to division (`./`) and exponentiation (`.^`). Whenever you operate on two same-size vectors **element-by-element**, the dot operator is required.

#### Example

Given `f = [1, 2, 3]` and `g = [4, 5, 6]`, then `f .* g` returns `[4, 10, 18]` — each pair multiplied independently.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- MATLAB signals are vectors; use `.*` to multiply them sample-by-sample, not `*`.
- The dot operator (`.*, ./, .^`) applies any operation element-by-element across two equal-length vectors.
- Omitting the dot causes a dimension error or a scalar result — a classic exam trap.

*In the next section, B.7-6 Matrix Operations, we move beyond row vectors to full matrices with arbitrary elements, where standard matrix multiplication rules apply.*
